frameworks.name: top-down vs bottom-up attention
frameworks.one_line: Interaction of goal-driven cueing and stimulus-driven salience in working memory

frameworks.name: visual working memory
frameworks.one_line: Limited-capacity short-term retention of visual items

frameworks.name: pre-cue attentional prioritization
frameworks.one_line: Informative cues bias encoding toward probable probe items

core_question: How do cue informativeness and probe status of salient items shape the interaction between top-down and bottom-up attention in visual working memory?
key_assumptions: Pre-cues operationalize top-down attention via probe-frequency expectations
Visual salience operationalizes bottom-up attention on memory items
Cue informativeness and probe status jointly modulate this interaction
manipulations: Visual salience of memory items varied
Probe status of salient items varied across three experiments
key_findings: Cueing benefits for cue-directed items observed consistently across experiments
Costs to non-cued items accompanied the cueing benefits
Cue informativeness emerged as a critical factor in the attention interaction
Probe status of salient items emerged as a critical factor in the interaction
interpretations: Top-down and bottom-up attention interact in VWM rather than acting independently
Cue informativeness and salient-item probe status jointly govern this interaction
